subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider an elementary reaction
$\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{D}(\mathrm{g})$
If the volume of reaction mixture is suddenly reduced to $\frac{1}{3}$ of its initial volume, the reaction rate will become ' $x$ ' times of the original reaction rate. The value of $x$ is :
question:
Consider an elementary reaction
$\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{D}(\mathrm{g})$
If the volume of reaction mixture is suddenly reduced to $\frac{1}{3}$ of its initial volume, the reaction rate will become ' $x$ ' times of the original reaction rate. The value of $x$ is :
solution:
Since, the reaction is elementary
$\text { Rate }=k[A]^1[B]^1$
When V is reduced to $\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~V}$, concentration will be tripled.
Hence, rate $=9 \times(\text { rate })_{\text {initial }}$
$x=9$

solution: The curve contain three activated complex as shown below in the curve.In the curve P and Q are the intermediates. The step with the highest activation energy is the rate determining step. Hence, in the given reaction, two intermediates, three activated complexes are involved and step-II is the rate determining step.

subject: Chemistry
q: The inactivation rate of a viral preparation is proportional to the amount of virus. In the first minute after preparation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the virus is inactivated. The rate constant for viral inactivation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Nearest integer) [Use : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mn>10</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>303</mn><mo>;</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>477</mn></math> property of logarithm<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open=""><mrow><mo>:</mo><mi>log</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mi>log</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The inactivation rate of a viral preparation is proportional to the amount of virus. In the first minute after preparation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the virus is inactivated. The rate constant for viral inactivation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Nearest integer) [Use : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mn>10</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>303</mn><mo>;</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>477</mn></math> property of logarithm<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open=""><mrow><mo>:</mo><mi>log</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mi>log</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
As the unit of rate constant is min-1 so it must be a first order reaction
k×t=2.303logA0/At
in 1 min 10% is inactivated so taking
A0=100   At=90 in 1 min
So k×1=2.303×log10090

=2.303×(log10-2log3)
=2.303×(1-2×0.477)
=0.10593

=105.93×10-3
Answer is 106

subject: Chemistry
q:
NO2 required for a reaction is produced by the decomposition of N2O5 in CCl4 as per the equation,
2N2O5g→4NO2g+O2g.
The initial concentration of N2O5 is 3.00 mol L-1 and it is 2.75 mol L-1 after 30 minutes. The rate of formation of NO2 is:
question:
NO2 required for a reaction is produced by the decomposition of N2O5 in CCl4 as per the equation,
2N2O5g→4NO2g+O2g.
The initial concentration of N2O5 is 3.00 mol L-1 and it is 2.75 mol L-1 after 30 minutes. The rate of formation of NO2 is:
solution:
Rate of reaction in terms of rate of disappearance of N2O5(s)
-dN2O5dt=12dNO2dt=2dO2dt=rate of Reaction
-(-3+1.75)30=rate of reaction
--0.2530=8.3×10-3mol L-1min-1
=253000=8.3×10-3=12× rate of formation of O2
8.3×10-3=12dNO2dt
1.66×10-2m/min =d[NO2]dt

subject: Chemistry
q:
The equation k=6.5×1012 s-1e-26000 K/T is followed for the decomposition of compound A. The activation energy for the reaction is ____ KJ mol-1. [nearest integer]

(Given: R=8.314 J K-1 mol-1)
question:
The equation k=6.5×1012 s-1e-26000 K/T is followed for the decomposition of compound A. The activation energy for the reaction is ____ KJ mol-1. [nearest integer]

(Given: R=8.314 J K-1 mol-1)
solution:
K=Ae-Ea/RT=6.5×1012 s-1e-26000 K/T

Ea8.314=26000

Ea=216.164 kJ/mol.

Hence,the activation energy of the given reaction is 216KJ mol-1

q:
For a chemical reaction A+B→ Product, the order is 1 with respect to A and B.
Rate
mol L-1 S-1		 [A]
mol L-1		 [B]
mol L-1
0.10 20 0.5
0.40 x 0.5
0.80 40 y

What is the value of x and y?
question:
For a chemical reaction A+B→ Product, the order is 1 with respect to A and B.
Rate
mol L-1 S-1		 [A]
mol L-1		 [B]
mol L-1
0.10 20 0.5
0.40 x 0.5
0.80 40 y

What is the value of x and y?
solution:
To determine the values of x and y, we can use the information provided and the rate expression for the given reaction, which is first order with respect to both A and B:

Rate r = K[A]1 [B]1

0.1 = K(20)1 (0.5)1 …(i) 0.40 = K(x)1 (0.5)1 …(ii)  0.80 = K(40)1 (y)1 …(iii)

From (i) and (ii)

x = 80

From (i) and (iii)

y = 2

subject: Chemistry
q:
The following results were obtained during kinetic studies of the reaction.

2A+B→ product
 
Experiment A in mol L-1 B  in mol L-1 Initial rate of reaction in mol L-1 min -1
I 0.10 0.20 6.93×10-3
II 0.10 0.25 6.93×10-3
III 0.20 0.30 1.386×10-2

The time (in minutes) required to consume half of A is
question:
The following results were obtained during kinetic studies of the reaction.

2A+B→ product
 
Experiment A in mol L-1 B  in mol L-1 Initial rate of reaction in mol L-1 min -1
I 0.10 0.20 6.93×10-3
II 0.10 0.25 6.93×10-3
III 0.20 0.30 1.386×10-2

The time (in minutes) required to consume half of A is
solution:
Let's assume x and y are the order of the reaction with respect to A and B, respectively.

From equation 1 and 2,

0.10.1x0.20.25y=6.93×10-36.93×10-3⇒y=0

From equation 1 and 3 and put the value y=0,

0.10.2x0.20.30=6.93×10-31.386×10-2    ∴y=0

12x=12⇒x=1

The rate law will be R=K[A]1[B]0

Hence, the reaction is of the first order.

K=RA=6.93×10-30.1=6.93×10-2

Here t12 for the first-order reaction is given by, t12=0.693K=0.693(6.93×10-2)=10

question: Assuming <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi>μg</mi></math> of trace radioactive element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> with a half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> years is absorbed by a growing tree. [Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mn>10</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>303</mn><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi>log</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn></math>]
solution:
λt=lnaa-x

we know that half life time t1/2 = ln2λ

So, λ = ln2t1/2

100=30ln2ln1w100=30log2log1w100=300.30log1wlog1w = 1

1w=10

W=0.1μg

=1×10-1μg

q: The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>14</mn></mmultiscripts><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>12</mn></mmultiscripts></mfrac></math> in a piece of wood is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> part that of atmosphere. If half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>14</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5730</mn></math> years, the age of wood sample is ..... years.
question: The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>14</mn></mmultiscripts><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>12</mn></mmultiscripts></mfrac></math> in a piece of wood is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> part that of atmosphere. If half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>14</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5730</mn></math> years, the age of wood sample is ..... years.
solution:
Given half life of C14= 5730 years.

λt=lnC14/C12atmosphere C14/C12wood sample 

As per the question,

C14/C12woodC14/C12atmosphere=18

So, λt=ln8

ln 2t1/2t=ln 8

t=3×t1/2=3×5730=17190 years

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct reaction profile diagram for a positive catalyst reaction.
question: The correct reaction profile diagram for a positive catalyst reaction.
solution:
Most of the chemical reactions have energy barrier. A positive catalyst in a chemical reaction increases the rate of reaction by lowering the energy barrier. It does not alter the energy of reactant as well as product.

In the above graph, the curve in the presence of catalyst is having less activation energy. The enthalpy change is constant after addition of the positive catalyst

Starting from an equimolar non-reactive mixture of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, the time taken for the concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> to become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> times that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>min</mi></math>. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
solution:
Given A=t1/2=54 min

B=t1/2=18 min

t=0 'x' M     t=0 'x' M

⇒ To calculate : At=16×Bt time =?

⇒ For I order kinetic : At=A02n

n→ no. of Half lives

⇒ Now from the relation (1)

⇒x2n1=x2n2×16⇒2n2=2n1×24

⇒n2=n1+4⇒tt1/22=tt1/21+4

⇒t118-154=4⇒t=4×18×5436

⇒t=108 min

subject: Chemistry
q:
NO2 required for a reaction is produced by decomposition of N2O5 in CCl4 as by equation 2N2O5( g)→4NO2( g)+O2( g)
The initial concentration of N2O5 is 3 mol L-1 and it is 2.75 mol L-1 after 30 minutes.
The rate of formation of NO2 is x×10-3 mol L-1 min-1, value of x is ________.
question:
NO2 required for a reaction is produced by decomposition of N2O5 in CCl4 as by equation 2N2O5( g)→4NO2( g)+O2( g)
The initial concentration of N2O5 is 3 mol L-1 and it is 2.75 mol L-1 after 30 minutes.
The rate of formation of NO2 is x×10-3 mol L-1 min-1, value of x is ________.
solution:
The given reaction is,

2 N2O5( g)→4NO2( g)+O2( g)

The initial concentration of N2O5 is 3 mol L-1 and it is 2.75 mol L-1 after 30 minutes.

Now, 

-ΔN2O5Δt=-(2.75-3)30-0 mol L-1 min-1=0.2530

12×ΔN2O5Δt=14 -ΔNO2Δt-ΔNO2Δt=0.2530×2=1.66×10-2 M/Min

≃17×10-3 M/Min

subject: Chemistry
q: For a reaction taking place in three steps at same temperature, overall rate constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfrac></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ea</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>Ea</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ea</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> respectively, the overall <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ea</mi></math> is _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math>.
question: For a reaction taking place in three steps at same temperature, overall rate constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfrac></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ea</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>Ea</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ea</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> respectively, the overall <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ea</mi></math> is _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math>.
solution:
According to Arrhenius concept,

K=Ae-Ea/RT

Given that,

K=K1.K2/K3

Using Arrhenius equation we can expand above equation as

e-Ea(net)/RT=e-Ea1/RT×e-Ea2/RTe-Ea3/RT

Hence,

 Ea(net)=Ea1+Ea2-Ea3⇒Ea(net)=40+50-60=30kJ/mol

subject: Chemistry
q:
The rate of a reaction doubles when its temperature changes from 300K to 310K. Activation energy of such a reaction will be:
​
R=8.314 JK-1mol-1 and log 2=0.301
question:
The rate of a reaction doubles when its temperature changes from 300K to 310K. Activation energy of such a reaction will be:
​
R=8.314 JK-1mol-1 and log 2=0.301
solution:
logk2k1=−Ea2.303R(1T2−1T1)

logk2k1=Ea2.303RT2-T1T1T2

log 2=Ea2.303×8.314×10300×310

Ea=0.301×2.303×8.314×300×31010=53603.9 J/mol

Ea=53.6 kJ mol-1.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Observe the following reactions at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$.
I. $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ products.
II. $5 \mathrm{Br}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{BrO}_{3}{ }^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Both the reactions are started at 10.00 am. The rates of these reactions at 10.10 am are same. The value of $-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}$ at 10.10 am is $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$. The concentration of A at 10.10 am is $10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. What is the first order rate constant (in $\mathrm{min}^{-1}$) of reaction $I$ ?
question:
Observe the following reactions at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$.
I. $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ products.
II. $5 \mathrm{Br}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{BrO}_{3}{ }^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Both the reactions are started at 10.00 am. The rates of these reactions at 10.10 am are same. The value of $-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}$ at 10.10 am is $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$. The concentration of A at 10.10 am is $10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. What is the first order rate constant (in $\mathrm{min}^{-1}$) of reaction $I$ ?
solution:
For reaction II:
5Br$^-$ + BrO$_3^-$ + 6H$^+$ $\rightarrow$ 3Br$_2$ + 3H$_2$O,
The overall reaction rate is:
$r = -\frac{1}{5}\frac{\Delta[Br^-]}{\Delta t} = -\frac{1}{3}\frac{\Delta[Br_2]}{\Delta t}$.
Given $\frac{\Delta[Br^-]}{\Delta t} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ mol L$^{-1}$ min$^{-1}$
The reaction rate is $r = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{5} = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ mol L$^{-1}$ min$^{-1}$.
Since the rates of both reactions are equal at 10.10 am and reaction I is first-order:
$r = k[A]$, so $4 \times 10^{-5} = k \times 10^{-2}$, giving $k = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ min$^{-1}$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following reaction
\[
\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}
\]
The time taken for \(\mathrm{A}\) to become \(1 / 4^{\text {th }}\) of its initial concentration is twice the time taken to become \(1 / 2\) of the same. Also, when the change of concentration of \(B\) is plotted against time, the resulting graph gives a straight line with a negative slope and a positive intercept on the concentration axis.
The overall order of the reaction is _______
question:
Consider the following reaction
\[
\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}
\]
The time taken for \(\mathrm{A}\) to become \(1 / 4^{\text {th }}\) of its initial concentration is twice the time taken to become \(1 / 2\) of the same. Also, when the change of concentration of \(B\) is plotted against time, the resulting graph gives a straight line with a negative slope and a positive intercept on the concentration axis.
The overall order of the reaction is _______
solution:
For $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction $75 \% \text { life }=2 \times 50 \% \text { life }$
So order with respect to A will be first order.
So order with respect to B will be zero. Overall order of reaction $=1+0=1$

question: The rate constant (k) of a reaction is measured at different temperature (T), and the data are plotted in the given figure. the activation energy of the reaction in kJmol-1 is : (R is gas constant)

In k=InA−EaR1T

Slope of graph 

=−EaR×103=−105

Ea=2R×103J

Ea=2R×kJ

subject: Chemistry
q: For a reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> the half lives are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> when the concentration of reactant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo>=</mo></math> respectively. The order of the reaction is
question: For a reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> the half lives are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> when the concentration of reactant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo>=</mo></math> respectively. The order of the reaction is
solution:
The relation between half-life and initial concentration of nth order reaction can be written as follows,

t12∝1A0n-1

100∝10.5n-1

50∝11n-1

21=10.5n-1

21=2n-1

n-1=1

n=2

order =2

For the second order reactions half-life is inversely proportional to initial concentration.

(II)

subject: Chemistry
q:
For $\mathrm{A}_2+\mathrm{B}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}$ for forward and backward reaction are 180 and $200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively
If catalyst lowers $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}$ for both reaction by $100 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
Which of the following statement is correct?
question:
For $\mathrm{A}_2+\mathrm{B}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}$ for forward and backward reaction are 180 and $200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively
If catalyst lowers $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}$ for both reaction by $100 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
Which of the following statement is correct?
options: Catalyst does not alter the Gibbs energy change of a reaction.
Catalyst can cause non-spontaneous reactions to occur.
The enthalpy change for the reaction is $+20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
The enthalpy change for the catalysed reaction is different from that of uncatalysed reaction.
solution:
$\mathrm{A}_2+\mathrm{B}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}=180 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{b}}=200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{b}}=-20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
In presence of catalyst :
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}=180-100=80 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{b}}=200-100=100 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$
Catalyst does not change $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ or $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ of a reaction.

subject: Chemistry
q:
A→BReactantProduct

If formation of compound B follows the first order of kinetics and after 70 minutes the concentration of A was found to be half of its initial concentration. Then the rate constant of the reaction is x×10-6 s-1. The value of x is____
question:
A→BReactantProduct

If formation of compound B follows the first order of kinetics and after 70 minutes the concentration of A was found to be half of its initial concentration. Then the rate constant of the reaction is x×10-6 s-1. The value of x is____
solution:
A→BReactantProduct

Since the reaction follows the first order kinetics and after 70 minutes the concentration of A was found to be half of its initial concentration. That means 70 minutes is the half life time for the reaction. And we know that for a first order reaction half life time is given as.

t12 = 0.693KK=0.693t12=0.69370×60 sec.

=69307×6×10-6 sec-1= 165 ×10-6 sec-1So, x = 165

subject: Chemistry
q:
The following mechanism has been proposed for the reaction of $\mathrm{NO}$ with $\mathrm{Br}_2$ to form $\mathrm{NOBr}$ : $\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Br}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NOBr}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
$$
\mathrm{NOBr}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NOBr}(\mathrm{g})
$$
If the second step is the rate determining step, the order of the reaction with respect to $N O(g)$ is
question:
The following mechanism has been proposed for the reaction of $\mathrm{NO}$ with $\mathrm{Br}_2$ to form $\mathrm{NOBr}$ : $\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Br}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NOBr}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
$$
\mathrm{NOBr}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NOBr}(\mathrm{g})
$$
If the second step is the rate determining step, the order of the reaction with respect to $N O(g)$ is
solution:
$$
\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Br}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NOBr}_2(\mathrm{~g})
$$
$$
\mathrm{NOBr}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NOBr}(\mathrm{g})
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{R} & =\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{NOBr}_2\right][\mathrm{NO}] \\
& =\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}[\mathrm{NO}]\left[\mathrm{Br}_2\right][\mathrm{NO}], \text { where } \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NOBr}_2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{Br}_2\right]} \\
& =\mathrm{K}^{\prime}[\mathrm{NO}]^2\left[\mathrm{Br}_2\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The decomposition of formic acid on gold surface follows first order kinetics. If the rate constant at 300 K is 1.0×10-3 s-1 and the activation energy Ea=11.488 kJ mol-1, the rate constant at 200 K is _________ ×10-5 s-1. (Round of to the Nearest Integer).

(Given R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1)
question:
The decomposition of formic acid on gold surface follows first order kinetics. If the rate constant at 300 K is 1.0×10-3 s-1 and the activation energy Ea=11.488 kJ mol-1, the rate constant at 200 K is _________ ×10-5 s-1. (Round of to the Nearest Integer).

(Given R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1)
solution:
K300=10-3   K200=?

Ea=11.488KJ/mole  R=8.314 J/mole-K

so ℓnK300 K200=EaR1200-1300

ℓnK300 K200=11.488×1000×1008.314×200×300

=2.303

=ℓn10

so K300 K200=10

K200=110×K300=10-4

=10×10-5 sec-1

subject: Chemistry
q:
The reaction 2NO+Br2→2NOBr takes place through the mechanism given below

NO+Br2⇌NOBr2 fast

NOBr2+NO→2NOBr slow

The overall order of the reaction is _____.
question:
The reaction 2NO+Br2→2NOBr takes place through the mechanism given below

NO+Br2⇌NOBr2 fast

NOBr2+NO→2NOBr slow

The overall order of the reaction is _____.
solution:
On applying Law of mass action over slowest step,

R=k[NOBr2][NO]

The slowest step is the rate determining step.

On applying Law of mass action on equilibrium of Step I

k=NOBr2NOBr2

NOBr2=kNOBr2

Hence, overall rate equation will be

R=kNO2Br2

=k'NO2Br2

The overall order is equal to sum of the powers of the concentration terms in the rate law.

The overall order of the reaction is 3.

question: The rate constant of a reaction increases by five times on increase in temperature from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>52</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The value of activation energy in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>kJmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is ___ .(Rounded-off to the nearest integer) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>314</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
T1=300 K, T2=325 K, K2=5 K

lnK2 K1=EaR1 T1-1 T2

or, ln5=Ea8.3141300-1325

or, Ea=0.7×2.303×8.314×12×325

=52271 J=52.271 kJ

Nearest integer answer will be 52 kJ

subject: Chemistry
q:
The reaction 2 A+B2→2AB is an elementary reaction.

For a certain quantity of reactants, if the volume of the reaction vessel is reduced by a factor of 3, the rate of the reaction increases by a factor of _____ . (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
question:
The reaction 2 A+B2→2AB is an elementary reaction.

For a certain quantity of reactants, if the volume of the reaction vessel is reduced by a factor of 3, the rate of the reaction increases by a factor of _____ . (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
solution:
Reaction : 2 A+B2⟶2AB

As the reaction is elementary, the rate of reaction is

r=K.[A]2 B2

on reducing the volume by a factor of 3, the concentrations of A and B2 will become 3 times and hence, the rate becomes 32×3=27 times of initial rate.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>(</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is a first order reaction. The initial pressure of the system was found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math> which increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math> after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math>. The total pressure of the system after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo></math>min will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math>. (Nearest integer)
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>(</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is a first order reaction. The initial pressure of the system was found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math> which increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math> after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math>. The total pressure of the system after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo></math>min will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math>. (Nearest integer)
solution:
The final pressure of each component can be calculated as follows,

                             A(g)→2 B( g)+C(g)intial                     800         0             0At time=t           800-p    2p           p

800 + 2p = 1600

2p = 800

p= 400

The first order reaction rate constant can be calculated as follows,

K=2.30310log800400=2.303×log210

For 30 min,

K=2.30330log(800)(800-y)

2.303×log210=2.30330log800800-y

⇒800800-y=8

100 = 800 – y

y = 700

Total pressure after 30 min

(800 – y) + (2y) + (y)

= 800 + 2y

= 800 + 1400

= 2200 mm Hg

q:
Consider the following reactions

The order of the above reactions are a, b, c and d,respectively. The following graph is obtained when log[rate] vs.log[conc.] are plotted:

question:
Consider the following reactions
A→P1;B→P2;C→P3;D→P4
The order of the above reactions are a, b, c and d,respectively. The following graph is obtained when log[rate] vs.log[conc.] are plotted:

  
Among the following, the correct sequence for the order of the reactions is :
solution:
Rate =K[Conc]order 

logRate =logK+ order× log[conc]
so slope of graph is order of reaction. greater the slope greater is order of reaction so order of reaction ⇒"c<a<b<d''
